serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of cooked mutton is approximately 100g, equivalent to a palm-sized portion of boneless cooked sheep meat.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked Mutton — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for cooked mutton (F06050006). Values reflect mature sheep meat cooked without bone. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb/mutton (NDB 17174) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions; these are documented as adapted values.

gi_source.title: N/A — Cooked mutton contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Cooked mutton contains 0g carbohydrate per 100g. Pure protein-and-fat foods do not elicit a meaningful glycemic response and are not assigned a GI value. glycemic_index is set to null (not 0) per the low/zero-carbohydrate GI rule. gi_category is set to not_applicable. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate content is zero.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high saturated fat content (~28.5g/100g) and trace trans fat (0.2g/100g) contributing to arterial stiffness and elevated cardiovascular risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit cooked mutton to small portions (60–80g) consumed infrequently, choose lean cuts such as leg of mutton, trim all visible fat before and after cooking, and prioritise lower-fat protein sources to reduce saturated fat intake per AHA dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high fat content (67.9g/100g, ~28.5g saturated fat) and trace trans fat (0.2g/100g) worsening insulin resistance despite zero carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although cooked mutton contains 0g carbohydrate and 0g sugar, its very high saturated fat and energy density (644 kcal/100g) worsen insulin resistance and promote dyslipidaemia; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 60g infrequently, choose very lean cuts, and trim all visible fat per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (~28.5g/100g), trace trans fat (0.2g/100g), and cholesterol (77mg/100g) significantly elevating LDL-cholesterol and cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit cooked mutton due to its very high saturated fat and trans fat content, which raise LDL-cholesterol and increase atherosclerotic risk; if consumed, restrict to very small portions (≤60g) of the leanest available cut, infrequently, per ACC/AHA heart-healthy dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat, arachidonic acid, and trace trans fat promoting pro-inflammatory cytokine activity (TNF-alpha, IL-6)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid cooked mutton during active flares and minimise consumption at other times, replacing it with anti-inflammatory proteins such as fatty fish, legumes, or tofu, as its high saturated fat and arachidonic acid content promote systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cooked mutton refers to mature sheep meat (from animals typically over 12 months old) that has been cooked by boiling, stewing, or braising until tender, without bone. It is a staple protein in Mamak cuisine in Singapore and Malaysia, as well as in South Asian, Middle Eastern, and North African culinary traditions. The meat has a firm, dense texture and a robust, gamey flavour that is more pronounced than lamb due to the animal's age and higher fat content. Per 100g, cooked mutton provides 644 kcal, 10.7g protein, 67.9g fat (including approximately 28.5g saturated fat and 0.2g trans fat), 0g carbohydrate, 0g sugar, 64mg sodium, 310mg potassium, 8mg calcium, 3.4mg iron, and 77mg cholesterol. The very high fat content — likely reflecting a fatty cut or rendered fat inclusion — makes this a high-energy, high-saturated-fat food. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Clinically, cooked mutton is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its extremely high saturated fat, trace trans fat, and pro-inflammatory fatty acid profile. Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — this is a pure protein-and-fat food with no carbohydrate content. Dietary_fibre_g and sugar_g are naturally zero as mutton contains no plant-derived components. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates.
common_user_queries: How many calories in cooked mutton?
Can I eat mutton with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (F06050006) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked lamb/mutton (NDB 17174), which is the closest approved-source equivalent. All adaptations are documented.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo source — cooked mutton contains no carbohydrate. Sugar_g is naturally zero as mutton contains no plant-derived sugars. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as mutton is an animal product with no plant fibre. Saturated fat (28.5g), monounsaturated fat (27.1g), polyunsaturated fat (4.8g), and trans fat (0.2g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17174 (cooked lamb, composite of retail cuts, cooked) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions. The very high energy density (644 kcal/100g) and fat content (67.9g/100g) suggest this record reflects a fatty cut or includes rendered fat. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g/100g); glycemic_index set to null and gi_category set to not_applicable per the low/zero-carbohydrate GI rule — setting glycemic_index to 0 would be incorrect for a food with no carbohydrate. Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB or USDA for this specific preparation): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific HPB cooked mutton record are not separately reported in HPB Foodo or confirmed in USDA SR Legacy for the exact preparation as of 2026.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is null (not 0 and not a string) — correct per the low/zero-carbohydrate GI rule for a food with 0g carbohydrate. gi_category is 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. gi_source is documented with full rationale. All four GI fields are present and correctly populated.

All three required health conditions addressed with specific clinical guidance. Aliases consistent between flat array and structured object. Ontology anchors defensible.
